item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) because the image was taken by one "Delores Johnson" as indicated here with no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
honestly loved the riff on "keter duty" in this one, which hints at an in-universe reason why it was removed. all without any LOLfoundation!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SCP. It feels very realistic (well, relatively at least), and thee framing works quite well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love the use of this fairly simple SCP as a springboard for a more in-depth story and meta-analysis. The whole thing feels wonderfully authentic. Believable human mistakes are made, and they're analyzed in a realistic, thoughtful way. While the phenomenon itself is entertaining, I feel like the real highlight here is this glimpse into the Foundation's workings, and the way it's made to feel like something operated by real human beings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sarah Palmer the taller black guy?

Huh?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. My only suggestion: change the title to "A Thing What Makes You Sarah Palmer"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. One of the best logs I've ever read. Thankfully you didn't spoiled it with "demoted to D-class" or other "Keter duty" bullshit, latter is even nicely lampshaded. Upvoting so hard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really great! The logs were well-done; a tactically sound operation, and believable bureaucratic administration. All excellently presented, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this is great.

This seems to be perfect for a crosstest with SCP-2140 (in fact is exactly the kind of anomaly that it is supposed to be able to counteract). Would it alter the past so that the exposed agent never entered the house? Change the events of Operation Snowglobe so the Foundation didn't screw it up? Change events after the conversion so that this particular Sarah Palmer has since gotten 2/2140 clearance? Or just make the original Sarah Palmer a Foundation employee with 2/2140 in the first place? Which is the path of least resistance, which is the most interesting story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would love to know more about the Sarah Palmers. Not saying the article needs more, because it's fantastic as it is, but all the information you've given (in such clever ways) just makes me think about how many different ways a base personality could diverge and how incredibly irritating it might be to live with people who have most, if not all, of your bad habits and annoying quirks.

And then the logs! This is exactly how operations go wrong in real life. What a disaster. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: question what happen to the extra sarah palings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The humanity of the overconfidence/overreaction really make this one something great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus. Christ.
How did I not read this earlier?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely the best recovery log I've ever read, though part of me feels like it's a bit long and could work better as a tale. Then again, the SCP isn't really complete without it, so it's not something that absolutely needs to happen or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh this is gorgeous. The effect was pretty standard but good on its own, but the site director's report makes it just awesome. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reads like a tale. It's fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done. I like the director's commentary on what went wrong with the recovery mission.

Also I love this:
"More commonly known as Sarah Palmer the Taller Black Guy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, changed it. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely minor nitpick but instead of saying that the original Sarah Palmer is "invulnerable" to SCP-2310 I think it would flow better to say she is "Immune" to it. To me invulnerability implies physical involvement.

Otherwise I really like this one +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Wouldn't it be easier to purchase nearby terrain with a shell company and go from there?"

Isn't the whole point of this SCP to point out that the easiest solution is not always the best solution?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I feel that's part of the humor.

Different strokes, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a refreshing read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is weird.

SCP-2150 has the same effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …. This is just me, but I find it peculiar that the skip maintains its anomolous properties after being deconstructed….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, this was funny. The Taller Black Guy, the cultist in robes that were woman's nightwear….I really liked it.

Even when it wasn't funny, it was good. The tone and purpose of the article were far different than anything else I've seen here. Having the recovery report analyzed was a very good idea.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I just finished the first season of The Wire so… it might not be completely coincidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I wondered if it might, and some other people feel that way, so I'll go back and give it a trim sometime soon.
2. Fair point— I think I'm going to change the moving process to "They pick up the entire house and drive it off," since that's apparently how houses get moved in real life.
3. Basically, yeah. The Foundation decided that putting her on the payroll and letting her tell the -As what reasonable people the Foundation are was cheaper than hiring extra guards.

Thanks for your thoughts!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taught me a new word ("cognomen"), which is always a good start.

I was afraid that the logs would go on too long for me, but in the end they stayed coherent and interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like how this deconstructs the whole "and then a bunch of agents died because somebody did something stupid" trope you see so often. Don't very often see any focus on that, let alone a good presentation of what would realistically happen in the aftermath of somebody royally screwing up like this. I particularly like how the original documentation shows how a mistaken assumption and personal bias colored the entire way that the following events were perceived, thanks to someone hearing hoofbeats and thinking horses instead of Ungulates.

Overall, makes for a very interesting, character focused story. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dumb actually gets used in a semi-official manner when you're talking about things like control systems or threat types. In that case dumb means its a thing that just does what it does blindly. A dumb security measure, for example, would be something like a razor wire barricade- it doesn't actively do anything, but it accomplishes its task simply by sitting there being pointy and hard to get around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the logs were the hook, they fell on their face for me. If I wanted to read how human people botch up operations, I'd check the news.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for identity-based SCP articles. This was pretty well done and conceptually interesting (particularly "Sarah Palmer the taller black guy"). However, my least favorite part of the article was the logs of Operation Snowglobe. It was reminiscent of SCP-1609 insofar as it was "here's how someone screwed the pooch dealing with an anomalous thing that they didn't understand."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's so difficult now to come up with an anomaly that hasn't already been done several times and done better

Essentially, this is SCP-963. Not the same story, no, not by a very long shot; but it's been done before (and as for who did it better, not going there).

The greatest assets here, though, are the outlook it gives us on the horror of cognitive hazards and the operative capacities of the Foundation. I don't like a few things in the article (that "not fit for her duties" cliché is really, really getting on my nerves, and the whole character of Press is plain unlikable to my Tastes; then again, it is just a document, not Prell), but with so much good, I would be insincere if I did not upvote this. :)

item.title: The post no one wants to see
item.content:encoded: Sarah Palmer the regenerating lizard, anyone?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this kind of thing is the way forward for SCP articles.

It's so difficult now to come up with an anomaly that hasn't already been done several times and done better, that the emphasis will shift from telling the story of the object to the story of the Foundation people involved.

A few small criticisms of this otherwise excellent entry:
(1) I thought the moralising tone went on for a little too long, and became finger-wagging, especially at the end.
(2) How did the Foundation know that the anomaly would move if the house was re-created somewhere else? What if it's tied to the building materials of the original? Or to the plot of land?
(3) So the original Sarah Palmer is working for the Foundation? What exactly is her job? Is she just a babysitter for her duplicates?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Series 3 title for this… leaves something to be desired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nicely done. I suggest changing footnote 6 to inline notes, breaking the transcript around it, just because of the "I" in there. Otherwise, this tells a great story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty excellent bit of storytelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to hear that. This is a lovely work and may well be my favorite this month.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honored to hear it, the Gulf Canon was a big influence on this and some other stuff I'm working on

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing the explanations you gave to Sound Chaser and this, i think i'll yield.

You have my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think you could do with a cover story, but alright, I'll acquiese. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure, but here's Wikipedia's article on the practice of moving whole buildings: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Structure_relocation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some anvils need to be dropped. I upvoted so hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Did they take gas and water pipes and all? 'Cause that still seems rather… difficult. Not to mention noticeable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Onsite containment doesn't make sense to me, given that it's located in a densely-populated neighborhood. You're right that underground is too far, though, that's been fixed and I've added a brief justification for moving it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anne Arundel is not a DC suburb, it's an Annapolis suburb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final note is a bit melodramatic in places, but the message it conveys and the moral learned therein is too great for me not to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like this. It's a nice look at the review system, although I do think that the logs were a bit over the top with regards to the "haha look at this dumbass", and I can't imagine the director calling the SCP "dumb", seems a bit childish. Then again, that could just be personal things, so I dunno.

One thing seriously bugs me, and that's that they stored the house underground. I mean, come on, how the hell would they manage that? Like Omniscientist said earlier, you can have onsite containment.

Neutral-voting for now. Fix things, or at least make them feasible, for upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anne Arundel is a wealthy DC suburb, and the house is in a densely-populated area— that's why so many 2310-As ended up in the building. It's cheaper and less attention-grabbing to move it than to buy up an entire inhabited neighborhood. I'll try to update the article to be clearer about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is like, exactly how my own headcannon (pew) Foundation would operate, would sound like, would look like, everything. This is one of the greatest things of realistic with anomalous I've ever read, like an episode of "The Wire" set in SCP universe

Surprise twist: "The Wire" is also set in Maryland

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At least for me, i think the biggest hole in the story is how the Foundation managed to remove the house from the county and then put it underground. Wouldn't it be easier to purchase nearby terrain with a shell company and go from there?

A lot of SCP objects that are located on fixated terrain are contained on site. An old example is 097 and a brand new one is Kate's 2972.

I'll keep myself neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a minor fix per te.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: The way I imagined it, they don't each get their own house, they share four houses. I'll update the article to make that clear. As for why they aren't in cells: partly because the Foundation relies on -A volunteers to research the SCP, partly because they feel bad for their fallen comrades, but mostly because the most secure prison is one the prisoners don't want to leave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edit: Ok, I'm pretty sure I agreed to review this on the forums, but that didn't happen. I apologize. However, this looks like it's doing fine. My only complaint would be that the -As each get their own house. Like, why not standard humanoid containment? Most humanoids live their lives as prisoners.
